tldr: Sift and Osso solve different problems — Sift is fraud and risk scoring, Osso is enterprise SSO (SAML/OIDC) glue — so this is not a like-for-like migration; treat it as decommissioning Sift and adopting Osso for a separate auth need.
why_switch: You actually need enterprise SSO (SAML/OIDC) for B2B customers, not fraud scoring
Osso abstracts per-tenant IdP configuration without custom code
Open-source heritage and freemium pricing lower entry cost vs full IdP suites
Fits SaaS adding enterprise-tier auth as a checkbox feature
Sift's fraud capabilities are not replaced by Osso — keep or replace separately if still needed
what_to_export: List of enterprise customers needing SSO and their IdP types (Okta, Azure AD, Google, etc.)
Existing user directory (emails, org membership, roles)
Any current SAML / OIDC metadata or certificates already in use
Session and login flow definitions in your app
From Sift: rules, lists, and historical events (only if you still need fraud scoring elsewhere)
Webhook / event endpoints currently consuming Sift decisions
Role and permission mappings tied to identity
export_method: Because Sift and Osso are different categories, there is no direct export from Sift to Osso. From Sift, pull configuration and historical decisions via its APIs and console for archival. For Osso onboarding, gather your customer IdP list and existing auth flow documentation from your own systems. Consult official Sift and Osso docs for current endpoints and onboarding flows.
import_method: Integrate Osso into your application's auth layer (typically via OAuth-style redirect plus per-tenant SAML/OIDC connection records you provision via Osso's API or dashboard). Coordinate with each enterprise customer to exchange SAML metadata or OIDC client details. Run a pilot with one customer before broad rollout. If you still need fraud detection, evaluate a dedicated fraud vendor in parallel — do not expect Osso to replace Sift's risk scoring.

concept_mapping.note: Different domain — keep or replace fraud scoring separately.
Osso provides authenticated identity, not a trust score.
Configuration shape is unrelated; recreate based on auth needs.
Different payloads; rebuild any downstream consumers.

gotchas: Osso does not replace fraud detection — if you turn off Sift, plan a separate fraud strategy
Per-tenant IdP onboarding has human latency; budget time for customer back-and-forth
SAML certificate rotation is a recurring operational cost
Audit and compliance teams may need to review the new auth path
Keep Sift running until the SSO rollout is complete to avoid mixing concerns mid-migration
Confirm Osso's current pricing and feature scope in official docs before committing

email.subject: Decommissioning Sift and adopting Osso for enterprise SSO
email.body:
Hi team,

Quick clarifier: Sift and Osso are not in the same category. Sift handles fraud and risk scoring; Osso handles enterprise SSO (SAML/OIDC). This work has two threads:

1) Adopt Osso for SAML/OIDC SSO so we can ship enterprise-tier auth without per-tenant custom code. Pilot with one customer, then roll out.
2) Decide separately on Sift — either keep it, replace it with another fraud vendor, or accept the gap if fraud is no longer in scope.

Estimated effort for the Osso side: 2-6 weeks depending on customer count. I'll circulate a runbook and customer-onboarding checklist.

Thanks,
